examples.code:
; Hello World Program
; Demonstrates basic string output using DOS interrupt 21h

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    MSG DB 'Hello, World!$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    ; Initialize data segment
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display string using DOS function 09h
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, MSG
    INT 21H
    
    ; Return to DOS
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: String must end with $ for DOS function 09h
MOV AX, @DATA loads the data segment address into AX, then copies to DS
AH=09h is DOS function to display string at DS:DX
LEA loads effective address of MSG into DX

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize DS before using string data
Not ending the string with '$' - function 09h uses $ as terminator
Using AH=01h instead of AH=09h for string output

examples.code:
; Basic Assembly Template
; Standard program structure for 8086 assembly

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Data segment variables go here
    
.CODE
MAIN PROC
    ; Initialize data segment
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Main program logic goes here
    
    ; Exit to DOS
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: .MODEL SMALL defines a small memory model (code + data < 64KB)
STACK 100H reserves 256 bytes for the stack
Always initialize DS before accessing data segment variables

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize DS register
Not ending program with INT 21h/AH=4Ch
Confusing .DATA and .CODE segment order

examples.code:
; Addition Program
; Demonstrates adding two numbers and storing the result

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    NUM1 DB 25
    NUM2 DB 17
    RESULT DB ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Load first number into AL
    MOV AL, NUM1
    
    ; Add second number
    ADD AL, NUM2
    
    ; Store result (CF will be set if overflow occurred)
    MOV RESULT, AL
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: ADD modifies the destination (AL), source (NUM2) unchanged
ADD sets CF=1 if unsigned overflow occurred
RESULT receives AL, which holds the sum

commonMistakes: Using ADD on two memory operands directly (one must be register)
Not checking CF after unsigned addition to detect overflow
Assuming result fits in destination when adding large numbers

examples.code:
; Buffered Input Example
; Reads a single character with buffer

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    CHAR DB ?
    PROMPT DB 'Enter a character: $'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display prompt
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, PROMPT
    INT 21H
    
    ; Read character (function 01h - echo input)
    MOV AH, 01H
    INT 21H
    MOV CHAR, AL
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: AH=01h reads one character from stdin into AL, also echoes to screen
Character returned in AL is stored to CHAR variable

commonMistakes: Using function 01h without checking if character was actually read
Not providing user feedback before reading input
Confusing AH and AL when setting up INT 21h functions

examples.code:
; Buffered String Input
; Reads a string using buffered input (function 0Ah)

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    BUFFER DB 80
           DB ?
           DB 80 DUP(?)
    PROMPT DB 'Enter your name: $'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display prompt
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, PROMPT
    INT 21H
    
    ; Read string using buffered input
    MOV AH, 0AH
    LEA DX, BUFFER
    INT 21H
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Buffer format: [MAX LENGTH][ACTUAL LENGTH][DATA...]
AH=0Ah reads string into buffer, Enter terminates

commonMistakes: Not reserving enough space in buffer for input
Forgetting that first byte is maximum length, second is actual length
Not initializing buffer before use

examples.code:
; Demo Program - Arithmetic and Comparison
; Shows basic arithmetic operations and conditional logic

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    A DB 10
    B DB 3
    RESULT DB ?
    MSG1 DB 'A > B$'
    MSG2 DB 'A <= B$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Compare A and B
    MOV AL, A
    CMP AL, B
    
    ; If AL > B, skip the subtraction
    JA SKIP_SUB
    
    ; Subtract: A - B
    SUB AL, B
    MOV RESULT, AL
    JMP DONE
    
SKIP_SUB:
    ; Add: A + B
    ADD AL, B
    MOV RESULT, AL
    
DONE:
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CMP performs subtraction but doesn't store result, only updates flags
JA jumps if unsigned AL > B (CF=0 and ZF=0)
SUB sets OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF based on result

commonMistakes: Confusing JA (unsigned) with JG (signed)
Forgetting that CMP only affects flags, doesn't store result
Using CMP then wrong jump condition

examples.code:
; Procedure Example
; Demonstrates procedure call with stack operations

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    RESULT DW ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Call the procedure
    MOV AX, 5
    PUSH AX              ; Pass parameter on stack
    CALL SQUARE
    POP CX               ; Clean up stack
    MOV RESULT, DX
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP

; Procedure to calculate square
; Parameter: AX (value to square)
; Returns: DX (result)
SQUARE PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    
    MOV AX, [BP+4]      ; Get parameter
    MUL AX               ; AX * AX -> DX:AX
    MOV DX, AX           ; Store result in DX
    
    POP BP
    RET
SQUARE ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH decrements SP by 2 and copies AX to stack
CALL pushes return address and jumps to SQUARE
MUL without operand uses AX as implicit operand
RET pops return address and jumps back to caller

commonMistakes: Not balancing stack - push/pop mismatch causes corruption
Forgetting to preserve BP register in procedure
Using AX instead of [BP+4] to access stack parameters

examples.code:
; For Loop Example
; Counts from 1 to 10 using LOOP instruction

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    COUNT DB 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Initialize counter
    MOV CX, 10          ; Loop 10 times
    
LOOP_START:
    INC COUNT           ; Increment count variable
    
    ; Could do more work here
    
    LOOP LOOP_START     ; CX--, if CX != 0 jump to LOOP_START
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CX is the counter register used implicitly by LOOP
LOOP decrements CX, jumps if CX != 0
INC adds 1 to operand, doesn't affect CF

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize CX before LOOP
Modifying CX inside the loop unexpectedly
Using LOOP with CX=0 (will loop 65536 times!)

examples.code:
; If-Else Example
; Demonstrates conditional branching

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    X DB 5
    Y DB 10
    MSG_EQ DB 'X equals Y$'
    MSG_NE DB 'X not equal to Y$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Compare X and Y
    MOV AL, X
    CMP AL, Y
    
    ; If equal, display equality message
    JE EQUAL
    
    ; Else branch - display not equal message
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, MSG_NE
    INT 21H
    JMP DONE
    
EQUAL:
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, MSG_EQ
    INT 21H
    
DONE:
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CMP sets flags based on AL - Y (result not stored)
JE jumps if ZF=1 (equal), otherwise continues
JMP unconditionally transfers control to label

commonMistakes: Forgetting that CMP doesn't store result
Using JE for signed comparison when JA/JB needed
Not using JMP to skip else block after then block executes

examples.code:
; Recursive Procedure Example
; Calculates factorial using recursion

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    RESULT DW ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Calculate 5! = 120
    MOV AX, 5
    PUSH AX
    CALL FACTORIAL
    POP AX
    MOV RESULT, DX
    
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP

; FACTORIAL procedure
; Input: n on stack (word)
; Output: DX = n!
FACTORIAL PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    
    MOV CX, [BP+4]      ; Get n
    
    ; Base case: if n <= 1, return 1
    CMP CX, 1
    JLE BASE_CASE
    
    ; Recursive case: n * factorial(n-1)
    PUSH CX
    DEC CX
    PUSH CX
    CALL FACTORIAL
    POP CX               ; Clean up parameter
    
    ; Multiply result by n
    MOV AX, DX
    MOV DX, [BP+4]
    MUL DX               ; AX * DX -> DX:AX
    
    POP BP
    RET
    
BASE_CASE:
    MOV DX, 1
    POP BP
    RET
FACTORIAL ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Stack frame preserves return address and BP
Base case prevents infinite recursion
Each recursive call pushes new return address

commonMistakes: Forgetting base case - causes infinite recursion and stack overflow
Not cleaning up stack parameters before RET
Losing track of which registers are preserved across calls

examples.code:
; Bubble Sort Example
; Sorts an array of bytes using bubble sort

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ARRAY DB 5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2
    N DB 6
    TEMP DB ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Outer loop: N-1 passes
    MOV CH, N
    DEC CH               ; Passes = N-1
    
OUTER_LOOP:
    MOV CL, N
    DEC CL               ; Comparisons per pass = N-1
    LEA SI, ARRAY        ; Point to start of array
    
INNER_LOOP:
    MOV AL, [SI]         ; Get current element
    CMP AL, [SI+1]       ; Compare with next
    JBE NO_SWAP          ; Skip if AL <= next
    
    ; Swap: exchange [SI] and [SI+1]
    MOV TEMP, AL
    MOV AL, [SI+1]
    MOV [SI], AL
    MOV AL, TEMP
    MOV [SI+1], AL
    
NO_SWAP:
    INC SI
    LOOP INNER_LOOP
    
    DEC CH
    JNZ OUTER_LOOP
    
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: [SI] uses register indirect addressing
JBE jumps if unsigned AL <= [SI+1]
Three MOVs required to swap values in memory

commonMistakes: Not decrementing comparison count in inner loop correctly
Using signed jump (JLE) instead of unsigned (JBE) for array indices
Forgetting that LOOP uses CX, corrupting outer loop counter

examples.code:
; Flag Instructions and Stack Operations
; Demonstrates flag manipulation and stack behavior

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    counter DW 0
    result DB 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Flag Instructions ===
    ; Clear carry flag for arithmetic
    CLC                 ; CF = 0
    
    ; Set direction flag for string operations (forward)
    CLD                 ; DF = 0 (SI/DI auto-increment)
    
    ; Example: Adding numbers without carry interference
    MOV AL, 0FFh        ; AL = 255
    ADC AL, 01h         ; AL = 0, CF was cleared so no add of 1
    
    ; Set carry flag for special conditions
    STC                 ; CF = 1
    
    ; === Stack Operations ===
    ; The stack grows toward lower addresses
    ; SP points to the current top of stack
    
    ; Push registers to preserve values
    PUSH AX             ; SP = SP - 2, [SP] = AX
    PUSH BX             ; SP = SP - 2, [SP] = BX
    PUSH CX             ; SP = SP - 2, [SP] = CX
    
    ; Use registers freely now
    MOV AX, 1234h
    MOV BX, 5678h
    ADD AX, BX          ; AX = 68ACh
    
    ; Pop in reverse order (LIFO)
    POP CX              ; CX = original CX, SP = SP + 2
    POP BX              ; BX = original BX
    POP AX              ; AX = original AX
    
    ; === Using BP for Stack Frame Access ===
    PUSH BP             ; Save caller's BP
    MOV BP, SP          ; BP points to current stack frame
    
    ; Access parameters at [BP+4] (return address) and [BP+6] (first param)
    MOV AX, [BP+4]      ; Get return address
    
    POP BP              ; Restore caller's BP
    
    ; === LAHF - Load Flags into AH ===
    ; LAHF copies SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF to AH bits 0-4
    LAHF                ; AH = flags register lower byte
    
    ; Check carry flag
    TEST AH, 01h        ; Test CF bit (AH bit 0)
    JNZ CARRY_SET       ; Jump if carry flag was set
    
CARRY_SET:
    ; Exit program
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CLC clears the carry flag to prepare for unsigned arithmetic
ADC adds with carry - since CF=0, this acts like regular ADD
PUSH decrements SP by 2 and copies source to [SS:SP]
Stack grows DOWN in memory - SP decreases with each push
POP increments SP by 2 and copies from [SS:SP] to destination
Must pop in reverse order (LIFO) to restore correctly
BP register provides stable reference to access stack parameters
LAHF loads lower 8 flags into AH for testing or saving

commonMistakes: Confusing SP and BP - SP tracks top of stack, BP is frame pointer
Popping in wrong order corrupts values - always use LIFO (reverse push order)
Forgetting that PUSH/POP operate on 16-bit words, not bytes
Not preserving BP before modifying it in a procedure

examples.code:
; Stack Instructions Demonstration
; Shows PUSH, POP, PUSHA, POPA, PUSHF, POPF operations

.386
.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    saved_ax DW 0
    saved_flags DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Basic PUSH/POP ===
    MOV AX, 1234h
    PUSH AX             ; Save AX
    MOV AX, 5678h       ; Modify AX
    POP AX              ; Restore AX (AX = 1234h again)
    
    ; === PUSHA/POPA - Push/Pop All General Registers ===
    ; Order: AX, CX, DX, BX, SP, BP, SI, DI
    MOV AX, 0AAAAh
    MOV CX, 0CCCCh
    MOV DX, 0DDDDh
    MOV BX, 0BBBBh
    MOV BP, 0EEEEh
    MOV SI, 0FFFFh
    MOV DI, 01234h
    
    PUSHA                ; Push all 8 registers (16 bytes)
    
    ; Modify all registers
    MOV AX, 0000h
    MOV CX, 0000h
    MOV DX, 0000h
    MOV BX, 0000h
    
    POPA                 ; Restore all registers
    ; AX = AAAA, CX = CCCC, etc.
    
    ; === PUSHF/POPF - Save and Restore Flags ===
    ; Set some flags via operations
    MOV AX, 0001h
    CMP AX, 0002h        ; ZF=0, CF=1 (AX < 2)
    PUSHF                ; Push flags to stack
    
    ; Clear flags
    CLC                 ; CF = 0
    CLD                 ; DF = 0
    
    ; Modify flags
    MOV BX, 0000h
    SUB BX, 0001h        ; CF = 1
    
    POPF                 ; Restore original flags (ZF=0, CF=1)
    
    ; === Complete Stack Frame Example ===
    PUSH BP              ; Save old BP
    MOV BP, SP           ; BP = current stack frame
    
    ; Stack now looks like:
    ; [BP]     = old BP
    ; [BP+2]   = return address
    ; [BP+4]   = first parameter (if any)
    
    SUB SP, 4           ; Allocate 4 bytes for local variables
    ; Local variables at [BP-2] and [BP-4]
    
    MOV [BP-2], AX      ; Store local var 1
    MOV [BP-4], BX      ; Store local var 2
    
    ; Restore stack frame
    MOV SP, BP           ; Deallocate locals
    POP BP               ; Restore old BP
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH preserves register values on stack for later restoration
PUSHA pushes all 8 general registers in specific order
Order: AX, CX, DX, BX, SP, BP, SI, DI (SP value is what was before decrement)
PUSHF saves current flag state to stack - useful before operations that modify flags
POPF restores flags from stack - restores exact flag state
Establishing stack frame with BP allows access to parameters and local variables
SUB SP, N allocates N bytes of local variable space

commonMistakes: PUSHA/POPA require .386 directive - MASM won't assemble without it
Forgetting that PUSH always decrements SP by 2 (for 16-bit)
POPA restores in reverse order but skips SP (restore order is DI, SI, BP, BX, DX, CX, AX)
Not balancing stack - too many PUSHes without POPs causes stack overflow
Using POP to restore flags when PUSHF wasn't called first

examples.code:
; Parameter Passing Demonstration
; Shows both register and stack parameter passing

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    result DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Method 1: Register Parameter Passing ===
    MOV AL, 5           ; First parameter in AL
    MOV BL, 3           ; Second parameter in BL
    CALL MULTIPLY_REG
    ; Result in AX = 15
    MOV result, AX
    
    ; === Method 2: Stack Parameter Passing ===
    PUSH 3              ; Push second parameter
    PUSH 5              ; Push first parameter
    CALL ADD_STACK
    ADD SP, 4           ; Clean up stack (2 params × 2 bytes)
    ; Result in AX = 8
    MOV result, AX
    
    ; === Caller-Saved Register Preservation ===
    PUSH CX             ; Save CX (caller saved)
    PUSH DX             ; Save DX (caller saved)
    
    MOV AL, 10
    CALL MULTIPLY_REG   ; CX, DX preserved across call
    
    POP DX              ; Restore DX
    POP CX              ; Restore CX
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h

; Register-based multiplication
; AL = first param, BL = second param
; Returns: AX = product
MULTIPLY_REG PROC
    MUL BL              ; AX = AL * BL
    RET
MULTIPLY_REG ENDP

; Stack-based addition
; Parameters at [BP+6] and [BP+4]
; Returns: AX = sum
ADD_STACK PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    
    MOV AX, [BP+6]      ; Get first parameter (pushed second)
    ADD AX, [BP+4]      ; Add second parameter (pushed first)
    
    POP BP
    RET                 ; Return address popped automatically
ADD_STACK ENDP

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Register parameter passing is fast but limited - only available registers
Stack parameters pushed in REVERSE order so first param is at higher address
CALL pushes return address before jumping to procedure
ADD SP, 4 cleans up stack (removes the 2 pushed parameters)
Caller-saved: calling code preserves registers it needs across calls
Stack frame: BP+6 is first param, BP+4 is second (due to reverse push order)
RET pops return address from stack and jumps back to caller

commonMistakes: Forgetting to clean up stack parameters after stack-based call (ADD SP, N)
Incorrect parameter offset calculation - [BP+4] for first, [BP+6] for second
Confusing caller-saved vs callee-saved register conventions
Not preserving registers when required by calling convention
Parameters pushed in wrong order causing offset confusion

examples.code:
; Array Manipulation Examples
; Shows how to define, address, and traverse arrays

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Byte array (8 elements)
    byteArray DB 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80
    
    ; Word array (4 elements)
    wordArray DW 1000h, 2000h, 3000h, 4000h
    
    ; Uninitialized byte array
    buffer DB 16 DUP(?)
    
    sum DW 0
    foundIndex DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Accessing Array Elements ===
    ; Direct indexing with constant offset
    MOV AL, byteArray[0]    ; AL = 10 (first element)
    MOV AL, byteArray[3]    ; AL = 40 (fourth element, offset = 3)
    
    ; Using register as index (SI, DI, BX)
    MOV SI, 2
    MOV AL, byteArray[SI]   ; AL = 30 (third element)
    
    ; === Word Array Indexing ===
    ; IMPORTANT: Offset is always in bytes, even for word arrays!
    MOV SI, 0
    MOV AX, wordArray[SI]       ; AX = 1000h (first word)
    
    MOV SI, 2                    ; SI = 2 (NOT 1 for second element!)
    MOV AX, wordArray[SI]        ; AX = 2000h (second word)
    
    MOV SI, 4
    MOV AX, wordArray[SI]        ; AX = 3000h (third word)
    
    ; === Searching an Array ===
    MOV CX, 8               ; Array length
    MOV SI, 0               ; Index
    MOV AL, 50              ; Value to find
    
search_loop:
    CMP AL, byteArray[SI]
    JE found
    INC SI
    LOOP search_loop
    
    ; Not found - SI = 8
    JMP done
    
found:
    MOV foundIndex, SI
    
done:
    ; === Summing an Array ===
    MOV CX, 8               ; Counter
    MOV SI, 0               ; Index
    XOR AX, AX              ; AX = 0 (accumulator)
    
sum_loop:
    ADD AL, byteArray[SI]
    INC SI
    LOOP sum_loop
    
    MOV sum, AX
    
    ; === Finding Max Value ===
    MOV CX, 8
    MOV SI, 0
    MOV AL, 0               ; Max = 0
    
max_loop:
    CMP byteArray[SI], AL
    JNA not_max
    MOV AL, byteArray[SI]
not_max:
    INC SI
    LOOP max_loop
    ; AL now contains maximum value (80)
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: byteArray[SI] uses SI as offset from array start - each element is 1 byte
Word array requires doubling index: element N at offset N*2
wordArray[2] accesses second element (2000h), not first (would be wordArray[0])
Loop compares each element to target value, exits when found or end reached
AL accumulator sums all bytes - note potential overflow for large arrays

commonMistakes: Forgetting that word array indices must be doubled (element 3 at offset 6, not 3)
Using byte array index for word array causing misalignment
Assuming byteArray[SI] modifies SI - it does NOT, just adds SI to base address
Memory-to-memory moves not allowed: MOV array[2], array[5] is ILLEGAL
Not using XOR AX, AX to clear accumulator - must initialize before ADD

examples.code:
; Number Input and Output
; Reads and prints multi-digit integers

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    prompt DB 'Enter a number (max 5 digits): $'
    buffer DB 7, ?, 7 DUP(?)
    number DW 0
    outputMsg DB 'You entered: $'
    newline DB 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display prompt
    LEA DX, prompt
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    
    ; Read string of digits
    LEA DX, buffer
    MOV AH, 0Ah
    INT 21h
    
    ; Convert ASCII string to number
    CALL ASCII_TO_NUM
    MOV number, AX
    
    ; Print result
    LEA DX, newline
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    
    LEA DX, outputMsg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    
    ; Print the number
    MOV AX, number
    CALL PRINT_NUM
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h

; ========================================
; Convert ASCII string in buffer to number
; Input: buffer contains digits ending with CR
; Output: AX = number
; ========================================
ASCII_TO_NUM PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    PUSH SI
    PUSH BP
    
    MOV SI, OFFSET buffer + 2  ; Point to first digit
    MOV CL, buffer[1]           ; Length in CL
    XOR CH, CH                 ; CX = length
    XOR AX, AX                 ; Result = 0
    MOV BP, 10                 ; Multiplier
    
convert_loop:
    CMP CX, 0
    JE convert_done
    
    MOV DL, [SI]              ; Get digit
    SUB DL, '0'               ; Convert ASCII to value
    
    ; AX = AX * 10 + digit
    MUL BP                    ; AX = AX * 10
    ADD AL, DL                ; Add digit
    
    INC SI
    LOOP convert_loop
    
convert_done:
    POP BP
    POP SI
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
ASCII_TO_NUM ENDP

; ========================================
; Print number in AX
; Algorithm: Divide by 10, push remainders, pop and print
; ========================================
PRINT_NUM PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    
    CMP AX, 0                 ; Handle zero specially
    JNE print_nonzero
    MOV DL, '0'
    MOV AH, 02h
    INT 21h
    JMP print_done
    
print_nonzero:
    XOR CX, CX                ; Digit counter
    
next_digit:
    CMP AX, 0
    JE print_digits
    
    MOV DX, 0
    MOV BX, 10
    DIV BX                    ; AX = quotient, DX = remainder
    
    ADD DL, '0'              ; Convert to ASCII
    PUSH DX                   ; Save digit
    INC CX
    JMP next_digit
    
print_digits:
    CMP CX, 0
    JE print_done
    
    POP DX                    ; Get digit
    MOV AH, 02h
    INT 21h                   ; Print character
    LOOP print_digits
    
print_done:
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
PRINT_NUM ENDP

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: buffer[1] contains the actual length of input (after function 0Ah reads)
MUL BP with BP=10 multiplies AX by 10 without destroying other registers
Special case: number is 0, just print '0'
DIV BX divides DX:AX by BX - quotient in AX, remainder in DX
Digits pushed to stack in reverse order, then popped to print in correct order
This is classic use of stack for reversal: LIFO gives reversed order

commonMistakes: Not converting ASCII '0'-'9' to values by subtracting '0' before arithmetic
Forgetting that DIV uses DX:AX - must clear DX before dividing AX by byte
Not handling the special case of number = 0 (would print nothing)
Buffer overflow: not reserving enough space in input buffer for digits
Confusing buffer format: buffer[0]=max, buffer[1]=actual, buffer[2]=first char

examples.code:
; Extending 8-bit Programs to 16-bit
; Shows how to preserve values and work with larger numbers

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    temp1 DW 0
    temp2 DW 0
    result DW 0
    value8 DB 200          ; 8-bit value
    value16 DW 60000       ; 16-bit value

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Problem: MUL overwrites AX and DX ===
    MOV AL, 100            ; 8-bit multiplicand
    MOV BL, 50             ; 8-bit multiplier
    
    PUSH AX               ; Save AX (contains AL)
    PUSH DX               ; Save DX (might be used elsewhere)
    
    MUL BL                ; AX = AL * BL = 5000 (1388h)
    ; AX = 1388h, DX = 0 (no high part overflow)
    
    POP DX                ; Restore DX
    POP AX                ; Restore AX
    
    ; === 16-bit Multiplication ===
    MOV AX, 1234h          ; Multiplicand
    MOV BX, 5678h          ; Multiplier
    
    PUSH AX               ; Save AX before MUL (MUL overwrites it)
    PUSH DX               ; Save DX (high part of result)
    
    MUL BX                ; DX:AX = AX * BX
    ; DX:AX now holds 32-bit result
    ; Result = 0x0BDC0F80 (approx 3.16 billion)
    
    POP DX                ; Restore DX
    POP AX                ; Restore AX
    
    ; === Division preserving registers ===
    MOV AX, 1234h         ; Dividend
    MOV CX, 100h          ; Divisor
    
    PUSH AX               ; Save dividend
    PUSH DX               ; Save any high part
    
    XOR DX, DX            ; Clear DX for 16-bit division
    DIV CX                ; AX = quotient, DX = remainder
    ; AX = 0x12 (18), DX = 0x34 (52)
    
    POP DX                ; Restore DX
    POP AX                ; Restore AX
    
    ; === Using Data Segment for preservation ===
    MOV AX, 0ABCDh        ; Important value
    MOV temp1, AX         ; Store in data segment
    
    ; Do something that modifies AX
    MOV BX, 1234h
    MUL BX                ; AX overwritten
    
    ; Restore original value
    MOV AX, temp1          ; AX = ABCDh again
    
    ; === 8-bit to 16-bit extension ===
    MOV AL, value8         ; AL = 200
    CBW                   ; Sign-extend AL to AX (AX = 0200h or FFE0h if negative)
    ; CBW: if AL < 128, AH = 00; if AL >= 128, AH = FF
    
    ; Now we can safely do 16-bit operations
    MOV BX, 1000h
    ADD AX, BX            ; AX = 0200h + 1000h = 1200h
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH before MUL preserves values that MUL would overwrite
MUL BL multiplies AL by BL, result in AX
16-bit MUL: DX:AX = AX * BX (32-bit result)
MUL always uses AX and another register - result splits across AX and DX
DIV overwrites AX and DX - must preserve if needed
XOR DX, DX clears DX before DIV to ensure high word of dividend is 0
Data segment storage preserves values across operations that modify registers
CBW sign-extends AL to AX - essential before 16-bit operations on 8-bit values

commonMistakes: Forgetting that MUL overwrites AX (and DX for 16-bit) - must preserve first
Not clearing DX before 16-bit DIV - DX:AX must be full dividend
Confusing CBW (sign-extend) with just moving AL to AH (which zeros AH)
Using stack for long-term storage - stack is for temporary preservation
Forgetting that PUSH/POP are 16-bit operations - cannot push 8-bit registers directly
